*The thing with the Internet generally and tech in general is you have to h=
ave some iteration cycle built in, and it=E2=80=99s a different world of ma=

oducts, I=E2=80=99m going to produce it.=E2=80=9D No, you have to launch pr=
oducts. Apple has a famous example of this with their services where they l=

to start but have an improvement function built in so you can iterate on it=
.*

ey did small town radio, and they worked their way up that way. On the Inte=
rnet, you just have to have this self-reinforcing iteration function to get=

The Art of the Take

*You used to have a podcast, I want to talk about one of your old ones, whi=
ch was =E2=80=9CThe Art of the Take=E2=80=9D.*

*SE:* Yes.

*We talk about takes a lot offline, but what is a take? How would you defin=
e it?*

*SE:* By the way, that podcast is off the Internet. You can find remnants o=
f it on the Internet, but I have all the episodes, we pulled it off. So The=
 =E2=80=9CArt of the Take=E2=80=9D, just real quickly was when I worked at =
WIP, our night show was this guy named Joe Giglio ( https://twitter.com/Joe=
GiglioSports ) and his producer was Jack Fritz ( https://twitter.com/JackFr=
itzWIP ). We would sit around for an hour before their show bouncing around=
 what his lead opinion would be for the show. Then we used to laugh and see=
 the big guns, the Stephen A. Smiths ( https://twitter.com/stephenasmith ) =
, the Skip Baylesses ( https://twitter.com/RealSkipBayless ) , the Colin Co=
wherds ( https://twitter.com/ColinCowherd ) of the world, and then we actua=
lly took it to politics too, which is why we were having fun. I don=E2=80=
=99t know if it would sound quite as fun listening to it now.-

*Right, some stuff doesn=E2=80=99t age.*

*SE:* Yes. So we would look at what they would say, and we would figure out=
 whether it was a good take or not. Our original definition was it would be=
 an opinion that would have 50% of the people agree with you and 50% of the=
 people disagree with you to start a conversation. If you could craft an op=
inion that did that, that was the way in to doing it. A lot of people say, =
=E2=80=9CWell, you are trolling when you=E2=80=99re doing that,=E2=80=9D an=
d I think some people are. But to me, it=E2=80=99s less about saying someth=
ing that you don=E2=80=99t believe that will do that, and more about thinki=
ng about all the things that you believe and picking the ones that do.

We used to have these rules where, =E2=80=9COkay, how much do you have to b=
elieve in a take to say it? Eh, 70%.=E2=80=9D So if you believe it 70% of t=
he way, then you should say it 100% of the way, because if you hedge and sa=
y, =E2=80=9CThis could happen,=E2=80=9D well anything could happen, but if =
you say, =E2=80=9CIt will happen,=E2=80=9D or, =E2=80=9CIs true,=E2=80=9D t=
hat=E2=80=99s a lot different than saying, =E2=80=9CCould be true.=E2=80=9D

So that=E2=80=99s what we would do, and we had lunatic listeners who came o=
ut with mathematical formulas for takes and all that kind of stuff. But to =
me that=E2=80=99s what sports talk is about, it=E2=80=99s about arguing abo=
ut who=E2=80=99s better. It takes you back to high school or middle school =
or a corner bar, and it=E2=80=99s why some of the long-lasting arguments or=
 the unsolvable ones, like LeBron versus Michael Jordan, because it could g=
o on and on and on and on and on forever. There=E2=80=99s plenty of people =
who think both sides, that=E2=80=99s why they last forever.

*Are takes universally beneficial or are they format-constrained? Because y=
ou have this bit about radio being interactive so you can give a take and p=
eople can call in and disagree. Whereas a podcast, it=E2=80=99s a niche. Yo=
u have to appeal to a relatively small number of people and do they want to=

=99s the first thing. I handle the podcast different than I handle radio. I=
 think there have been times where my nature is to say things declaratively=
, =E2=80=9CThat guy sucks.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CThat guy=E2=80=99s great.=E2=
=80=9D It=E2=80=99s just my nature. I would notice sometimes that our liste=
ners, if I was too negative too much, they didn=E2=80=99t like that very mu=
ch.

But I still think part of =E2=80=94 I=E2=80=99m sorry I=E2=80=99m jumping a=
round here =E2=80=94 I still think having a specific opinion is meaningful.=
 You either want the person who heard you to steal it and use it in an argu=
ment about the same thing because they agree with you, or you want them to =
bring it up to somebody and say, =E2=80=9CDo you believe how full of shit s=
o-and-so is?=E2=80=9D

*Right.*

*SE:* That=E2=80=99s the goal. I still think it=E2=80=99s magic on podcasts=
, but I do think having that balance because not everybody on a podcast is =
like that, and a lot of people who go to podcasts, I think, don=E2=80=99t r=
eally want that thing. My co-host is not like that, my co-host is not decla=
rative. I have to push him into making a choice, which keeps me on the othe=
r side. So I think balance is important. It=E2=80=99s a little bit differen=
t on podcasts. It=E2=80=99s not universal, I don=E2=80=99t think. I don=E2=
=80=99t think everybody wants it all the time.

*Yeah, I would say, I admire the art of the take, just broadly speaking. Li=
ke people who can drop great takes and you can share a tweet and say, =E2=
=80=9CWow, this is a great take,=E2=80=9D in that I don=E2=80=99t agree wit=

=E2=80=9D. It=E2=80=99s not possible, and I worry that I can never be a gre=

utation on trying to be right as often as possible. When I make a mistake, =
I feel like I have to rake myself over the coals to explain why I got it wr=

Stars and Customer Acquisition

*I was going to ask you about whether the future is multi-format, or is the=

=E2=80=99s constrained there? But this almost makes me think that no, the I=

 just to go back, I said it doesn=E2=80=99t matter if you=E2=80=99re right =
or wrong, the politician we were talking about does not seem to =E2=80=94

By the way, I look at how we do what we do. There=E2=80=99s a certain thing=
 in sports radio where everybody does a four-hour show, or most people do a=
 three-hour show or a four-hour show, and we just accept that as the norm. =
Maybe I=E2=80=99ve said this a bunch of times, and I don=E2=80=99t know it=
=E2=80=99s the case immediately, but maybe if I=E2=80=99m paying somebody t=

The other thing that you realize having done it is that there is a breaking=

*SE:* I did Afternoon Drive, which was 2:00 PM to 6:00 PM for four months o=
nce in Philadelphia with two other hosts. This one host named Josh Innes an=
d Hollis Thomas, who used to play for the Eagles. Josh was the primary host=

e whatever position I needed to take based on what Josh said. So if he said=
 X, even if I didn=E2=80=99t think Y, I would present the case of Y to try =
to make the show go along. Because of that, we would argue a lot.

I remember I was speaking to a class at St. Joe=E2=80=99s University, and o=
ne of the people asked, they said, =E2=80=9CHey, you and Josh sound like yo=
u hate each other. Do you guys hate each other?=E2=80=9D I said, =E2=80=9CH=
ere=E2=80=99s what I want you to do tomorrow.=E2=80=9D I said, =E2=80=9CI w=
ant you to argue=E2=80=A6=E2=80=9D Sam Bradford at the time was quarterback=

 Bradford for three hours. You say he=E2=80=99s good, then your friend says=

own this person since you were a child. Argue about Sam Bradford for three =
hours and go to bed, wake up, call him and argue with him for Sam Bradford =
for another three hours of everything that you just talked about the day be=
fore that you never resolved, and did this every day. You tell me how you f=
eel about your friend after a month. You probably need a break from them. I=
t might sound like to people that you guys hated each other. It=E2=80=99s j=
ust the reality of it.=E2=80=9D

You=E2=80=99re right, that marriage counseling of hosts is a very important=
 job that I have, making sure that they understand. I always tell them, =E2=
=80=9CYour job is not to convince the other person that you are right. Your=

ncedes, if he goes, =E2=80=9CYeah, you know what? You=E2=80=99re right=E2=
=80=9D, we=E2=80=99re 45 minutes into a four-hour show, we=E2=80=99re screw=
ed. The goal isn=E2=80=99t to win, the goal is to show the argument. The go=
al is to fight, not to win.

ous, and his podcast also is enormous. So I think sometimes the medium can =
